textbook: Complete Biology for Cambridge Secondary 1 — Pam Large, OUP

curriculum.subject: Biology
curriculum.subtopics: The seven life processes (MRS GREN): Movement, Respiration, Sensitivity, Growth, Reproduction, Excretion, Nutrition; differences between living and non-living things
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
What is Biology?
Biology is the scientific study of living things. Everything around us can be divided into two groups — living things (organisms) and non-living things. But how do we know if something is alive?

 The Seven Life Processes — MRS GREN
All living things carry out SEVEN life processes. ...
curriculum.activity: MRS GREN Card Sort: Write each life process on a card. Students sort picture cards of objects (fire, cloud, snail, tree, car, seed, crystal) into 'living', 'non-living', and 'once-living'. For each object, students tick which of MRS GREN it performs. Discuss borderline cases (virus, fire).
curriculum.homework: List five living things you saw today. For each one, give ONE example of how it shows any two life processes. Write in a table: Organism | Life Process 1 | Life Process 2 | Evidence.

curriculum.subject: Biology
curriculum.topic: Cells — The Basic Unit of Life
curriculum.subtopics: Animal cell structure; plant cell structure; differences between animal and plant cells; functions of cell organelles
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
What is a Cell?
A cell is the smallest unit of life. All living things are made of cells. Some organisms (like bacteria and amoeba) are made of just ONE cell — these are called unicellular organisms. Most living things we see (humans, trees, fish) are made of MILLIONS of cells — these are multicellu...
curriculum.activity: Microscope / Drawing Activity: If microscopes are available, prepare onion (plant) and cheek (animal) cell slides. Students draw and label what they see. If no microscopes, provide printed micrograph images and students label the structures. Use pencil, ruler, clear lines — no shading in scientific drawings.
curriculum.homework: Draw and fully label BOTH an animal cell and a plant cell. Write the function of each labelled structure beside it. Then write two sentences explaining why a plant cell needs a chloroplast but an animal cell does not.

curriculum.subject: Biology
curriculum.subtopics: Specialised cells and their adaptations; from cell to tissue to organ to organ system; examples in humans and plants
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Levels of Organisation
Living things are organised in levels of increasing complexity:

CELL → TISSUE → ORGAN → ORGAN SYSTEM → ORGANISM

 Level  Definition  Example (Human)  Example (Plant) 
--------------------------------------------------
 Cell  Basic unit of life  Red blood cell  Palisade cell 
...
curriculum.activity: Specialised Cell Poster: In pairs, students choose one specialised cell (e.g., red blood cell, root hair cell). They draw it large on A4 paper, add labels, write the function, and draw arrows to each adaptation explaining WHY that feature helps the cell do its job.
curriculum.homework: Explain what is meant by the term 'specialised cell'. Give TWO examples of specialised cells, and for EACH, describe one structural adaptation and explain how it helps the cell carry out its function. (6 marks)

curriculum.subject: Biology
curriculum.subtopics: Parts of the light microscope; how to use a microscope safely and correctly; preparing slides; making scientific drawings
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
The Light Microscope
The microscope is one of the most important tools in biology. It allows us to see structures too small to see with the naked eye.

 Parts of the Microscope and Their Functions

 Part  Function 
---------------
 Eyepiece lens  The lens you look through; usually ×10 magnification ...
curriculum.activity: Microscope Practice: Students prepare a wet mount of onion epidermal cells (or a prepared slide of letter 'e' or cotton fibres). They use the microscope to locate, focus, and sketch the image. They write a proper scientific drawing with labels and calculate the magnification used.
curriculum.homework: Calculate the total magnification for the following combinations: (a) eyepiece ×10, objective ×4; (b) eyepiece ×10, objective ×10; (c) eyepiece ×10, objective ×40. Then list FOUR rules a biologist must follow when making a scientific drawing.

curriculum.subject: Biology
curriculum.topic: Nutrition in Humans — Food and Nutrients
curriculum.subtopics: The seven food groups: carbohydrates, proteins, fats, vitamins, minerals, water, fibre; sources and functions of each; balanced diet
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Why Do We Need Food?
Food provides our bodies with nutrients. A nutrient is a substance in food that the body needs to:
- Provide energy
- Build and repair tissues
- Regulate body processes

 The Seven Food Groups

 1. Carbohydrates
- Sources: Rice, bread, yam, cassava, sugar, pasta, plantain
- Func...
curriculum.activity: Healthy Plate Analysis: Students bring (or draw from memory) their previous day's meals. Using a printed food group chart, they classify each food item, then evaluate whether their diet was balanced. They identify any missing nutrients and suggest one food they could add to each missing group.
curriculum.homework: Design a one-day meal plan (breakfast, lunch, dinner, snacks) for a 13-year-old in Ibadan that provides all seven nutrients. Present it in a table with columns: Meal | Food Item | Nutrient(s) Provided | Function. Explain why you included one protein source.

curriculum.subject: Biology
curriculum.subtopics: The human digestive system (organs and their functions); physical and chemical digestion; enzymes; absorption and egestion
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
What is Digestion?
Digestion is the process of breaking down large, insoluble food molecules into small, soluble molecules that can be absorbed into the blood.

Why must food be broken down? Food molecules (starch, proteins, fats) are too large to pass through the gut wall into the blood. They must ...
curriculum.activity: Model Digestion: Fill a zip-lock bag with soggy bread and a little water — this represents the stomach. Students 'churn' the bag. They then push the mixture through a stocking/mesh — this represents absorption through the small intestine. Discuss what got through and why. Connect to molecular size.
curriculum.homework: Draw and label the human digestive system. For each organ, state ONE function. Then answer: Why must food be digested before it can enter the blood? What is the difference between digestion and absorption? (4 marks)

curriculum.subject: Biology
curriculum.subtopics: Photosynthesis as a process; word equation; conditions needed; reactants and products; the leaf as an organ for photosynthesis
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
What is Photosynthesis?
Photosynthesis is the process by which green plants make their own food (glucose) using sunlight energy, water, and carbon dioxide.

Plants are autotrophs (self-feeders) — they make their own organic food. Animals are heterotrophs — they must eat other organisms for food.

 T...
curriculum.activity: Leaf Starch Test (Iodine Test for Photosynthesis): Use leaves that have been kept in light vs dark for 24 hours. Students perform the standard test (boil in water → boil in ethanol → rinse → add iodine). Light leaf turns blue-black (starch present). Dark leaf stays brown (no starch). Students record and explain.
curriculum.homework: Write the word equation for photosynthesis. List FOUR conditions needed for photosynthesis to occur. Draw a simple diagram of a leaf cross-section and label THREE structures that help the leaf carry out photosynthesis, explaining how each one helps.

curriculum.subject: Biology
curriculum.topic: Nutrition in Plants — Minerals and Water Uptake
curriculum.subtopics: Role of minerals (nitrates, phosphates, magnesium, calcium); how plants absorb water and minerals; active transport vs osmosis
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Why Do Plants Need Minerals?
Glucose from photosynthesis provides carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen. But plants also need minerals for other functions.

 Key Minerals and Their Functions

 Mineral  Function  Deficiency Symptom 
------------------------------------
 Nitrates (NO₃⁻)  Making amino acids and...
curriculum.activity: Osmosis in Potatoes: Cut potato chips of equal size. Place one in water, one in strong salt solution, one in sugar solution. Leave 30 minutes. Students measure and compare before/after lengths and explain results using osmosis. Write observations and conclusions.
curriculum.homework: Explain why a plant deficient in nitrates would show stunted growth. Describe what is meant by osmosis and explain how root hair cells are adapted to absorb water from the soil. Include a simple diagram of a root hair cell in your answer. (5 marks)

curriculum.subject: Biology
curriculum.subtopics: Aerobic respiration — equation, location, importance; anaerobic respiration; difference between breathing and respiration; uses of energy in the body
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
What is Respiration?
Respiration is the chemical process of releasing energy from food (glucose) in living cells. It occurs in EVERY living cell, ALL the time.

⚠️ Do NOT confuse respiration with breathing. Breathing is a physical process of moving air in and out of the lungs. Respiration is a chemi...
curriculum.activity: Yeast and Respiration: Mix warm water, sugar, and yeast in a bottle. Fit a balloon over the top. Students observe the balloon inflate over 15-20 minutes as CO₂ is produced by yeast fermentation. Discuss anaerobic respiration and applications (bread, beer). Record observations.
curriculum.homework: Write the word equation for aerobic respiration. Explain why muscle cells contain many mitochondria. Describe what happens in the muscles during intense exercise when not enough oxygen is available. What is the difference between respiration and breathing? (5 marks)

curriculum.subject: Biology
curriculum.subtopics: Structure of the human breathing system; the mechanism of breathing (inhalation and exhalation); gas exchange in the alveoli; adaptations of alveoli
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
The Breathing System vs the Respiratory System
- Breathing system = the organs involved in moving air in and out of the body (physical process)
- Respiratory system = sometimes used to mean the same thing, but strictly refers to the process of respiration (chemical, in cells)

 Structures of the Bre...
curriculum.activity: Bell Jar Model of Breathing: Make a simple model using a plastic bottle (cut bottom), two balloons inside (lungs), one balloon stretched over the bottom (diaphragm). Pull the bottom balloon down → air enters inner balloons. Push up → air leaves. Students label the model with real anatomical terms.
curriculum.homework: Describe what happens to the ribcage and diaphragm when you breathe in. Explain why alveoli are well adapted for gas exchange — give FOUR adaptations and explain each. (6 marks)

curriculum.subject: Biology
curriculum.subtopics: Functions of the skeleton; types of bones; joints — synovial, fixed, cartilaginous; antagonistic muscle pairs; tendons and ligaments
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Functions of the Skeleton
The human skeleton has FIVE main functions:

 Function  Explanation  Example 
------------------------------
 Support  Provides framework for the body  Spine holds body upright 
 Protection  Protects soft organs  Skull protects brain; ribcage protects heart and lungs 
 Move...
curriculum.activity: Arm Model: Students make a simple arm model using two cardboard tubes (bones), a brass fastener (joint), rubber bands (muscles), and string (tendons). Pull the 'biceps' string → arm bends. Pull 'triceps' string → arm straightens. Students draw and label their model.
curriculum.homework: List FIVE functions of the skeleton and give an example for each. Explain what is meant by an antagonistic muscle pair. Using the example of the biceps and triceps, describe what happens when you LIFT a heavy book and when you PUT IT DOWN. (6 marks)

curriculum.subject: Biology
curriculum.subtopics: Double circulation; the heart — structure and function; blood vessels (artery, vein, capillary); the cardiac cycle; heart rate and pulse
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Why Do We Need a Circulatory System?
Cells need a constant supply of oxygen and nutrients and need waste products removed. The circulatory system is the body's transport network.

 Double Circulation
Humans have double circulation — the blood passes through the heart TWICE for every complete circuit...
curriculum.activity: Pulse Rate Investigation: Students measure resting pulse rate (30 seconds × 2). Then exercise (jumping jacks) for 1 minute. Measure pulse immediately after, then every minute for 5 minutes. Record in a table and plot a line graph. Discuss: why does pulse rate increase with exercise?
curriculum.homework: Explain why the left ventricle has a thicker wall than the right ventricle. Compare arteries, veins, and capillaries — give THREE differences. Describe what happens in ONE complete heartbeat (cardiac cycle). (6 marks)

curriculum.subject: Biology
curriculum.subtopics: Composition of blood: plasma, red blood cells, white blood cells, platelets; functions of each component; blood types (briefly); the immune response
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
What is Blood?
Blood is a tissue — it is a liquid connective tissue that transports substances around the body. It makes up about 8% of body weight.

 The Four Components of Blood

 1. Plasma (~55% of blood)
- What: Pale yellow liquid
- Composition: Mostly water (~90%), with dissolved substances
- T...
curriculum.activity: Blood Component Proportions: Using measuring cylinders and coloured liquids (yellow for plasma, red food colouring + water for RBCs, colourless for WBCs/platelets), students mix in correct proportions to make 100ml 'blood'. They label each component and state its function.
curriculum.homework: Describe how a red blood cell is adapted for carrying oxygen. Give THREE adaptations. Explain how white blood cells help protect the body — describe the roles of BOTH phagocytes and lymphocytes. What is the role of platelets in wound healing? (6 marks)

curriculum.subject: Biology
curriculum.subtopics: Excretion vs egestion; organs of excretion (lungs, skin, liver, kidneys); the kidney — structure and function; urine formation; kidney disease briefly
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
What is Excretion?
Excretion is the removal of metabolic waste products — substances produced by chemical reactions inside the body.

⚠️ Excretion ≠ Egestion. Egestion is the removal of undigested food (faeces) that was never absorbed into the body's cells. Excretion is specifically the removal of M...
curriculum.activity: Kidney Model or Dialysis Demonstration: Fill a visking tubing (dialysis membrane) with a mixture of glucose solution and starch solution. Place in water for 20 minutes. Test the water outside for glucose (Benedict's) and starch (iodine). Glucose diffuses out (small molecule); starch stays in (large). Relate to filtration in kidney.
curriculum.homework: Explain the difference between excretion and egestion. Describe the THREE stages of urine formation in the kidney. Why is glucose not normally found in urine, even though it is filtered from the blood? What disease might cause glucose to appear in urine? (5 marks)

curriculum.subject: Biology
curriculum.subtopics: Central nervous system (CNS) and peripheral nervous system (PNS); neurones — sensory, relay, motor; reflex arc; reflex vs voluntary action; the brain
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
The Nervous System — Overview
The nervous system detects stimuli (changes in the environment) and coordinates responses. It has two parts:

- Central Nervous System (CNS): Brain and spinal cord — the processing centre
- Peripheral Nervous System (PNS): All the nerves connecting the CNS to the rest o...
curriculum.activity: Reaction Time Ruler Drop: Student A holds a 30cm ruler vertically between Student B's fingers (not holding). A drops without warning. B catches it. Measure where caught → convert to reaction time using a table. Repeat 5 times. Calculate average. Test how distraction (answering questions) affects reaction time.
curriculum.homework: Trace the pathway of a reflex arc for touching a hot surface. For each step (receptor, sensory neurone, relay neurone, motor neurone, effector), state what it is and what it does. Explain why reflex actions are important for survival. (5 marks)

curriculum.subject: Biology
curriculum.subtopics: Endocrine glands and their hormones; adrenaline and the fight-or-flight response; insulin and glucagon — blood glucose regulation; comparing nervous and hormonal communication
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
What is the Endocrine System?
The endocrine system uses hormones (chemical messengers) to coordinate long-term changes in the body. Hormones are produced by endocrine glands and travel in the blood.

Hormone: A chemical substance produced by an endocrine gland, transported in the blood, and which af...
curriculum.activity: Blood Glucose Simulation: Give students a bag of 'glucose tokens' (paper squares). Simulate eating (add tokens), then exercise (remove tokens). Students act as 'insulin' or 'glucagon' — deciding when to add/remove tokens to/from a 'glycogen store'. Record glucose levels and draw a graph.
curriculum.homework: Describe the effects of adrenaline on the body. Explain why these changes are useful. Then explain how insulin and glucagon work TOGETHER to keep blood glucose at a normal level. Draw a simple diagram showing the negative feedback loop. (6 marks)

curriculum.subject: Biology
curriculum.subtopics: Male and female reproductive organs; the menstrual cycle; fertilisation; early embryo development; the placenta and fetal development
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Reproduction
Reproduction is the production of new individuals of the same species. Humans reproduce sexually — this involves the joining of TWO sex cells (gametes).

 The Male Reproductive System

 Organ  Function 
----------------
 Testes  Produce sperm (from puberty) and testosterone 
 Epididymis...
curriculum.activity: Placenta Exchange Simulation: Two groups represent mother's blood and fetal blood. Using coloured cards for O₂, CO₂, glucose, urea, students pass the correct substances across a 'membrane' (a line of desks) in the correct direction. Discuss what the placenta allows and what it blocks.
curriculum.homework: Describe what happens at ovulation. Explain what happens if a sperm successfully fertilises the egg. Describe the functions of the placenta during pregnancy. Why does the placenta not allow the mother's blood to mix directly with the fetal blood? (5 marks)

curriculum.subject: Biology
curriculum.subtopics: Stages of human development (childhood, puberty, adolescence, adulthood, old age); puberty changes in males and females; growth graphs and growth rates; growth in other organisms (insects, plants)
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Stages of Human Life
Humans go through several distinct stages of growth and development:

 Stage  Age (approx.)  Key Features 
----------------------------------
 Infancy  0–2 years  Rapid growth; totally dependent 
 Childhood  2–10 years  Continued growth; language and motor skills develop 
 Puber...
curriculum.activity: Growth Measurement: Students measure their own heights and hand spans. Record class data. Calculate mean height for the class. Compare males vs females. Draw a bar chart of height distribution. Discuss growth spurt patterns and relate to the graph of human growth over a lifetime.
curriculum.homework: List FOUR physical changes that occur in MALES during puberty and FOUR in FEMALES. Explain why these changes occur (which hormone causes them). Sketch and label a typical human growth curve, marking the two main growth spurts.

curriculum.subject: Biology
curriculum.subtopics: Types of disease (communicable vs non-communicable); pathogens — bacteria, viruses, fungi, protozoa; transmission of disease; the body's defences; common diseases and their prevention
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
What is Health?
Health is a state of complete physical, mental, and social wellbeing — not just the absence of disease (WHO definition).

Disease is any condition that impairs normal functioning of the body or mind.

 Types of Disease

 Communicable (Infectious) Diseases
Caused by pathogens (disease...
curriculum.activity: Disease Transmission Simulation: Students move around shaking hands. One student is secretly 'infected' (has chalk/powder on hands). After several handshakes, students test hands under UV light or check for chalk. Trace who was infected and when. Discuss exponential spread and prevention (handwashing breaks the chain).
curriculum.homework: List the FOUR types of pathogen and give ONE example of a disease caused by each. Describe THREE of the body's natural defences against infection. Explain the difference between communicable and non-communicable diseases, giving an example of each. (5 marks)

curriculum.subject: Biology
curriculum.subtopics: How vaccines work; herd immunity; types of immunity (natural/artificial, active/passive); antibiotics and antibiotic resistance; recreational and medicinal drugs; effects of smoking and alcohol
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Vaccination
A vaccine contains weakened, killed, or inactivated forms of a pathogen (or parts of one). When injected, the immune system responds:
1. Antigens in vaccine detected → lymphocytes produce antibodies
2. Memory cells formed
3. If real pathogen enters later → memory cells → RAPID, large ant...
curriculum.activity: Smoking and Lung Model: Students attach a lit cigarette (or incense stick) to a syringe connected to cotton wool. 'Breathe' 20 times. Students observe the brown tar residue on cotton wool. Discuss what happens to the actual lungs. Alternatively, analyse real NHS/WHO data on smoking and health outcomes.
curriculum.homework: Explain how a vaccine protects the body against future infection. Use the terms: antigen, antibody, memory cell. Distinguish between active and passive immunity. Explain why antibiotics do NOT work against influenza. Describe TWO harmful effects of smoking on the body. (6 marks)

curriculum.subject: Biology
curriculum.subtopics: Chromosomes, genes, and DNA; genotype vs phenotype; dominant and recessive alleles; homozygous and heterozygous; monohybrid crosses; Punnett squares
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
What is Genetics?
Genetics is the study of heredity — how characteristics are passed from parents to offspring.

 DNA, Genes, and Chromosomes
- DNA (Deoxyribonucleic acid): The molecule that carries genetic information; found in the nucleus
- Chromosome: A long molecule of DNA coiled around proteins...
curriculum.activity: Coin Toss Genetics: Each coin represents one allele (heads = B, tails = b). Two students each toss a coin — combine results to make an 'offspring' genotype. Repeat 20 times. Record genotype and phenotype results. Compare observed ratios with predicted 3:1. Discuss probability and sample size.
curriculum.homework: In pea plants, tall (T) is dominant over short (t). Show, using a Punnett square, the offspring expected from crossing a homozygous tall plant (TT) with a short plant (tt). State the genotypic and phenotypic ratios. Then show the cross between two heterozygous tall plants (Tt × Tt). (6 marks)

curriculum.subject: Biology
curriculum.subtopics: Codominance; sex determination (XX and XY); sex-linked inheritance; genetic disorders — sickle cell anaemia, cystic fibrosis; carriers
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Codominance
In codominance, BOTH alleles are expressed in the phenotype — neither is dominant over the other.

Example: ABO blood groups (simplified)
- Ia (blood type A allele) and Ib (blood type B allele) are codominant
- ii (blood type O) is recessive to both

 Genotype  Blood Type 
--------------...
curriculum.activity: Family Tree (Pedigree) Analysis: Provide a pedigree chart for a family with cystic fibrosis or sickle cell anaemia. Students identify: affected individuals, carriers, likely genotypes for each person. Present findings to the class. Discuss the ethical issues of genetic testing.
curriculum.homework: Explain what is meant by a 'carrier' of a genetic disease. A couple are both carriers of sickle cell anaemia (HbAHbS). Draw a Punnett square to show their possible offspring. State the probability of each child: (a) being unaffected; (b) being a carrier; (c) having the disease. (5 marks)

curriculum.subject: Biology
curriculum.subtopics: Continuous vs discontinuous variation; genetic variation vs environmental variation; causes of variation — mutation, meiosis, sexual reproduction; measuring and representing variation
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
What is Variation?
Variation is the differences that exist between individuals of the same species.

No two individuals (except identical twins) are exactly alike. Variation is essential for evolution.

 Types of Variation

 1. Continuous Variation
- Characteristics that show a RANGE of values betwe...
curriculum.activity: Measuring Continuous Variation: Students measure their own handspan (cm). Record class data. Group into intervals (e.g., 15–17 cm, 17–19 cm, etc.). Tally frequency. Draw a histogram. Discuss: Is the distribution roughly normal (bell-shaped)? Compare males and females. Discuss genetic vs environmental factors.
curriculum.homework: Explain the difference between continuous and discontinuous variation. Give TWO examples of each. Describe THREE causes of genetic variation between individuals. Explain why identical twins, despite having the same DNA, may differ in some characteristics. (5 marks)

curriculum.subject: Biology
curriculum.subtopics: Darwin's theory of evolution by natural selection; evidence for evolution; adaptation; antibiotic resistance as evolution in action; selective breeding
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
What is Evolution?
Evolution is the gradual change in the inherited characteristics of a population over many generations. It explains why living things today are different from ancient ancestors and how new species form.

 Darwin's Theory of Natural Selection (1859)
Charles Darwin proposed that evo...
curriculum.activity: Peppered Moth Simulation: Make 'moths' from white and dark paper. Spread them on (a) white paper and (b) dark paper. Students act as 'birds' (predators) removing moths for 30 seconds. Count survivors. Repeat several 'generations'. Students see how selection changes population colour over time. Relate to industrial melanism.
curriculum.homework: Explain Darwin's theory of natural selection in your own words, using a specific example (e.g., giraffe's neck). Explain how antibiotic resistance in bacteria is an example of natural selection. What is the difference between natural selection and selective breeding? (5 marks)

curriculum.subject: Biology
curriculum.subtopics: Ecosystem, habitat, population, community, biotic and abiotic factors; food chains and food webs; trophic levels; energy flow; pyramids of numbers and biomass
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Key Ecological Terms

 Term  Definition  Example 
--------------------------
 Habitat  The place where an organism lives  A pond, a rainforest floor 
 Population  All individuals of ONE species in an area  All tilapia fish in a pond 
 Community  All the different species living together in an area  ...
curriculum.activity: Food Web Construction: Give students picture cards of organisms from a local ecosystem (grass, termites, lizard, eagle, snake, grasshopper, rat, hawk). Students arrange them and draw arrows (energy flow direction). Then remove one organism and trace the effects on the whole web. Discuss stability and biodiversity.
curriculum.homework: Draw a food web with at least 5 organisms. Identify one food chain within it with 4 trophic levels. Explain why a pyramid of biomass is almost always smaller at the top than at the bottom. If the eagle population in a food web is removed by hunting, predict what might happen to the snake and grasshopper populations. (5 marks)

curriculum.subject: Biology
curriculum.subtopics: The carbon cycle; the nitrogen cycle; the water cycle; decomposers and their role; human impact on cycles (deforestation, fossil fuels, fertilisers)
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Why are Cycles Important?
Atoms (carbon, nitrogen, water) are constantly recycled through living and non-living parts of the ecosystem. Without recycling, atoms would become permanently locked up and life could not continue.

 The Carbon Cycle

DIAGRAM: The Carbon Cycle
Draw arrows showing: CO₂ in a...
curriculum.activity: Carbon Cycle Role Play: Assign students roles — CO₂ molecule, plant, herbivore, carnivore, decomposer, fossil fuel, factory. Students hold 'carbon tokens' and move them as they follow instructions (photosynthesis, eating, death, burning). Record where carbon spends most time. Discuss how human activities disturb the cycle.
curriculum.homework: Draw and explain the carbon cycle. Include at least FOUR processes and indicate the direction carbon moves in each. Explain what would happen to atmospheric CO₂ if ALL decomposers were destroyed. Describe ONE way that humans are disturbing the carbon cycle and explain its consequence. (5 marks)

curriculum.subject: Biology
curriculum.subtopics: Pollution (air, water, land); deforestation and habitat destruction; endangered species; extinction; conservation strategies; sustainable development
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Human Impact on Ecosystems
Human activities are causing serious damage to natural ecosystems. The main threats are:

 1. Pollution

 Type  Sources  Effects 
----------------------
 Air pollution  Burning fossil fuels, vehicle exhaust  Acid rain, smog, global warming (CO₂), ozone layer damage (CFCs) ...
curriculum.activity: Local Ecosystem Audit: Take students outside (school compound or nearby area). Students identify and list: 5 plants, 5 animals, signs of pollution or habitat change. On return, map their findings and write a brief report: What is the biodiversity like? Are there any threats? What conservation action could the school take?
curriculum.homework: Describe THREE ways in which deforestation harms the environment. Explain what is meant by 'biodiversity' and why it is important. Describe TWO conservation strategies that could be used to protect an endangered animal species, and explain how each one works. (6 marks)

curriculum.subject: Biology
curriculum.subtopics: Why we classify; Linnaeus and binomial nomenclature; the five kingdoms; vertebrate and invertebrate groups; dichotomous keys
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Why Do We Classify Organisms?
Classification is the grouping of organisms based on their similarities and differences.

Reasons for classification:
- Makes communication easier (scientists worldwide use the same names)
- Shows evolutionary relationships
- Helps predict characteristics of newly disco...
curriculum.activity: Dichotomous Key: Provide picture cards of 8 local organisms (insects, birds, plants). Students work in groups to design a dichotomous key that correctly identifies ALL 8. They test each other's keys by seeing if a partner can use the key to correctly identify each organism.
curriculum.homework: Explain what is meant by the binomial system of naming organisms. Write the scientific name of a lion correctly. Describe THREE differences between a reptile and a mammal. Draw a simple dichotomous key that could be used to identify 4 different organisms of your choice. (6 marks)

curriculum.subject: Biology
curriculum.subtopics: Xylem and phloem — structure and function; transpiration; stomata and guard cells; cohesion-tension theory (simplified); translocation
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Transport in Plants
Plants need a transport system to:
- Move water and minerals from roots to leaves (xylem)
- Move sugars (made in leaves) to all other parts (phloem)

 Xylem
- Function: Transports water and dissolved minerals from roots to leaves
- Direction: Upward only (unidirectional)
- Struct...
curriculum.activity: Transpiration Investigation: Set up two identical leafy shoots — one with leaves covered in petroleum jelly (lower surfaces), one normal. Place in identical conditions (same volume of water, same light). Measure water uptake daily using a ruler to measure the meniscus in a calibrated tube. Compare rates. Discuss results.
curriculum.homework: Describe the differences between xylem and phloem in terms of: structure, function, and direction of transport. Explain what is meant by transpiration. State TWO factors that would increase the rate of transpiration and explain why each increases it. (5 marks)

curriculum.subject: Biology
curriculum.subtopics: Review of all Year 1 topics: cells and organisation, nutrition, respiration, circulatory system, excretion, nervous system, genetics, variation, ecology, classification, plant biology
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Year 1 — Key Concepts Summary

 Unit 1: Cells and Organisation
- MRS GREN — 7 life processes
- Animal vs plant cell differences
- Specialised cells and their adaptations
- Levels: cell → tissue → organ → system → organism

 Unit 2: Nutrition and Digestion
- 7 food groups and their functions
- Human ...
curriculum.activity: End-of-Year Relay Quiz: Divide class into teams. Teacher asks questions from each topic area. Teams confer and write answers on whiteboards or paper. First correct answer wins a point. Cover all major topics. Students identify gaps in their knowledge and create a personal revision list for areas they found difficult.
curriculum.homework: Complete the practice exam questions above under timed conditions (40 minutes). Review your answers using your notes and identify THREE topics you need to revise most. Create a one-page summary of your THREE weakest topics using mind maps or revision cards.

curriculum.subject: Biology
curriculum.subtopics: Why cells divide; the cell cycle; stages of mitosis (PMAT); importance of mitosis in growth, repair, and asexual reproduction; cancer as uncontrolled mitosis
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Why Do Cells Divide?
All new cells come from existing cells. Cells divide for three main reasons:
1. Growth — producing more cells to increase body size
2. Repair and replacement — replacing worn-out or damaged cells (skin cells, red blood cells)
3. Asexual reproduction — some organisms reproduce by...
curriculum.activity: Chromosome Model: Give students different-coloured strips of pipe cleaners or card (each colour = one chromosome). Students arrange them to show each stage of mitosis: chromosomes condense, line up, separate, new nuclei form. Then 'divide' the cell with a line. Check: each new 'cell' has the same number.
curriculum.homework: Explain why it is important that mitosis produces genetically identical daughter cells. Describe what happens at EACH stage of mitosis (prophase, metaphase, anaphase, telophase). If a cell has 46 chromosomes before mitosis, how many chromosomes will be in each daughter cell? Explain your answer. (5 marks)

curriculum.subject: Biology
curriculum.subtopics: Purpose of meiosis; stages of meiosis (overview); comparison with mitosis; gametes and fertilisation; genetic variation through meiosis
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
What is Meiosis?
Meiosis is a type of cell division that produces sex cells (gametes) — sperm and egg cells (and pollen in plants).

Meiosis occurs in the reproductive organs: testes (sperm), ovaries (eggs), anthers (pollen).

 Why is Meiosis Different from Mitosis?
Sexual reproduction requires two ...
curriculum.activity: Mitosis vs Meiosis Comparison Chart: Students draw two parallel diagrams — one for mitosis, one for meiosis — using coloured pencils to show chromosomes. They annotate key differences at each stage. Then write a paragraph explaining why meiosis is essential for maintaining the correct chromosome number across generations.
curriculum.homework: Explain why meiosis is described as a 'reduction division'. A cell with 46 chromosomes undergoes meiosis. How many chromosomes are in each resulting cell? How does crossing over during meiosis contribute to genetic variation? List FOUR differences between mitosis and meiosis. (6 marks)

curriculum.subject: Biology
curriculum.subtopics: DNA double helix structure; base pairing rules; DNA replication; genes and protein synthesis (overview of transcription and translation); mutations
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
DNA Structure
DNA (Deoxyribonucleic Acid) is the molecule of heredity. It stores all the instructions for building and running an organism.

 The Double Helix
DNA is shaped like a twisted ladder (double helix), with:
- Two strands running antiparallel (in opposite directions)
- The 'rails' of the la...
curriculum.activity: DNA Replication Model: Use cardboard bases cut into complementary shapes (A fits T, G fits C). Students 'unzip' a model DNA strand and assemble two new complementary strands. Label original and new strands. Then decode a short mRNA sequence into amino acids using a provided codon table.
curriculum.homework: Draw and label the structure of a DNA molecule, showing the bases, sugar, and phosphate groups. Apply base pairing rules to complete this strand: ATCGGA → __. Explain what is meant by 'semi-conservative replication'. Describe the two stages of protein synthesis in the correct order. (5 marks)

curriculum.subject: Biology
curriculum.subtopics: What is genetic engineering; recombinant DNA technology; examples (insulin production, GM crops); ethical issues; selective breeding revisited; stem cells
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
What is Genetic Engineering?
Genetic engineering (genetic modification) is the direct manipulation of an organism's DNA to change its characteristics.

Scientists can:
- Remove a gene from one organism
- Insert it into another organism (often a bacterium or plant)
- The receiving organism then expre...
curriculum.activity: GM Debate: Split class into two groups. One group argues FOR a specific GM crop (e.g., Golden Rice or drought-resistant maize for Nigeria). The other argues AGAINST. Each group researches and presents a 3-minute case. Class votes on the most convincing argument and discusses the importance of evidence-based decision making.
curriculum.homework: Describe the steps involved in producing human insulin using genetic engineering. Include the terms: restriction enzyme, plasmid, sticky ends, DNA ligase, recombinant DNA. State TWO advantages of this method over earlier methods of obtaining insulin. Give ONE ethical concern about genetic engineering. (6 marks)

curriculum.subject: Biology
curriculum.subtopics: What is homeostasis; negative feedback explained; thermoregulation in humans; control of body temperature (nervous and hormonal); osmoregulation (revisited); blood glucose (revisited)
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
What is Homeostasis?
Homeostasis is the maintenance of a constant internal environment despite changes in external conditions.

The body must regulate:
- Body temperature (37°C in humans)
- Blood glucose concentration
- Water content (osmolarity)
- Blood pH, CO₂ concentration

 Negative Feedback — T...
curriculum.activity: Thermoregulation Model: In groups, students produce a flow diagram for BOTH the 'too hot' and 'too cold' negative feedback loops for body temperature. Include: stimulus → receptor → coordinator → effectors → response → reversal. Present their diagrams and explain each step. Discuss what happens if homeostasis fails (fever, hypothermia).
curriculum.homework: Explain what is meant by negative feedback and why it is important for homeostasis. Describe in detail what happens in the body when you become very hot. Include at least THREE specific responses and explain how each one helps to cool the body. (5 marks)

curriculum.subject: Biology
curriculum.subtopics: Asexual reproduction in plants (runners, bulbs, tubers, cuttings, tissue culture); sexual reproduction — flower structure; pollination (insect and wind); fertilisation; seed dispersal; germination
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Asexual Reproduction in Plants
Plants can reproduce without seeds or flowers using vegetative propagation:

 Method  Example  How 
----------------------
 Runners (stolons)  Strawberry, spider plant  Horizontal stems grow along ground → new plant forms where they touch soil 
 Bulbs  Onion, garlic, t...
curriculum.activity: Flower Dissection: Obtain a simple flower (hibiscus, lily, or local garden flower). Students carefully dissect and identify each part. They mount parts on paper, label each one, and state its function. Sketch the cross-section. If flowers aren't available, use a detailed diagram and clay model-making instead.
curriculum.homework: Compare insect-pollinated and wind-pollinated flowers — give FOUR differences. Describe what happens from the moment a pollen grain lands on the stigma to the formation of a seed. Name THREE methods of seed dispersal and describe ONE adaptation for each method. (6 marks)

curriculum.subject: Biology
curriculum.subtopics: Diffusion; osmosis; active transport — comparison; exchange surfaces and their adaptations; surface area to volume ratio; examples in biology
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Three Methods of Transport Across Membranes

 Process  Definition  Energy?  Direction 
--------------------------------------
 Diffusion  Movement of molecules from HIGH to LOW concentration  No (passive)  With concentration gradient 
 Osmosis  Diffusion of WATER through a semi-permeable membrane fr...
curriculum.activity: SA:V Ratio Experiment: Cut agar (dyed with indicator, e.g., phenolphthalein) into cubes of different sizes (1cm, 2cm, 3cm). Place in acid solution. Measure time for acid to diffuse to centre (colour change). Record results. Calculate SA:V for each cube. Conclude: smaller cubes → faster diffusion → higher SA:V. Relate to cells and organisms.
curriculum.homework: Explain why a small single-celled organism does not need a transport system, but a large multicellular organism does. Compare diffusion and active transport in terms of: energy requirement, direction of movement, and examples. Describe FOUR characteristics of an efficient exchange surface. (5 marks)

curriculum.subject: Biology
curriculum.subtopics: Enzymes as biological catalysts; enzyme structure; lock-and-key model; factors affecting enzyme activity (temperature, pH); enzyme denaturation; enzymes in industry
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
What are Enzymes?
Enzymes are biological catalysts — they speed up chemical reactions in living organisms without being used up or changed themselves.

- Made of protein
- Each enzyme catalyses only ONE type of reaction (they are specific)
- Found in all living cells; also secreted by cells (e.g., d...
curriculum.activity: Enzyme Experiment: Students test the effect of temperature on amylase. Prepare starch solution in 3 water baths (cold ~10°C, body temp ~37°C, hot ~60°C). Add amylase to each. At 1-minute intervals, take drops and test with iodine on a white tile. Record time until iodine no longer turns blue-black (all starch digested). Compare rates. Plot results.
curriculum.homework: Explain the lock-and-key model of enzyme action. Draw a simple diagram to support your answer. Describe what happens to an enzyme when the temperature is raised well above its optimum. Why can this process not be reversed? Explain why pepsin works best in the stomach and salivary amylase works best in the mouth. (5 marks)

curriculum.subject: Biology
curriculum.subtopics: Light-dependent and light-independent reactions (overview); chloroplast structure; factors limiting photosynthesis; investigating photosynthesis rates; the role of glucose in plants
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Overview of Photosynthesis Stages
Photosynthesis occurs in TWO main stages inside the chloroplast:

 1. Light-Dependent Reactions (in thylakoid membranes)
- Light energy is absorbed by chlorophyll
- Water is split (photolysis): H₂O → H⁺ + O₂ + electrons
- ATP is produced (light energy → chemical ene...
curriculum.activity: Elodea/Pondweed Investigation: Place pondweed in a beaker of water. Position a lamp at set distances (5cm, 10cm, 15cm, 20cm). Count O₂ bubbles per minute at each distance. Record in a table. Plot a graph (bubbles/min vs distance). Identify the limiting factor at each point. Extend: add bicarbonate and observe change.
curriculum.homework: Explain what is meant by a 'limiting factor' for photosynthesis. Using a graph, explain what would happen to the rate of photosynthesis if you increased light intensity but kept CO₂ at a very low level. Describe how a greenhouse farmer could increase the rate of photosynthesis in their crops. (5 marks)

curriculum.subject: Biology
curriculum.subtopics: Detailed aerobic respiration pathway (glycolysis, Krebs cycle overview); measurement of respiration rate; respiratory quotient (RQ); exercise and respiration; Practical investigation
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Detailed Aerobic Respiration Pathway

 Stage 1: Glycolysis (in Cytoplasm)
- Glucose (6 carbons) → split into 2 molecules of Pyruvate (3 carbons each)
- A small amount of ATP produced
- Occurs in the cytoplasm
- Does NOT require oxygen

 Stage 2: Link Reaction (in Mitochondrial Matrix)
- Pyruvate → A...
curriculum.activity: Germinating Seeds Respirometer: Place freshly germinating seeds (e.g., mung beans) in a sealed boiling tube or jar with a capillary tube attached. Soda lime absorbs CO₂. Mark starting position of liquid in capillary tube. After 20-30 mins, measure how far the liquid has moved (O₂ consumed). Calculate rate per gram per hour. Compare with dead seeds as control.
curriculum.homework: Name the FOUR stages of aerobic respiration and state WHERE in the cell each stage occurs. A student measures that a small mammal consumes 50ml of O₂ and produces 50ml CO₂ per hour. Calculate the RQ. What does this tell you about the mammal's respiratory substrate? Explain why breathing rate increases during exercise. (5 marks)

curriculum.subject: Biology
curriculum.subtopics: Population growth curves (J-shaped and S-shaped); carrying capacity; factors affecting population size (birth rate, death rate, immigration, emigration); predator-prey relationships
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Population Growth
A population is a group of individuals of the same species living in the same area at the same time.

Population size changes due to:
- Birth rate (natality): Number of new individuals added per unit time
- Death rate (mortality): Number dying per unit time
- Immigration: Individua...
curriculum.activity: Population Growth Simulation: Give students a starting 'population' of 4 counters. Each counter reproduces: doubles each round if food tokens are available. Place limited food tokens on the table. Each round: students match counters to food tokens (those without food tokens 'die'). Track population over 10 rounds. Plot graph and identify carrying capacity.
curriculum.homework: Explain what is meant by 'carrying capacity'. Describe the THREE phases of an S-shaped (sigmoidal) population growth curve, giving a reason for each phase. Explain why, in a predator-prey relationship, the predator population always LAGS BEHIND the prey population. (5 marks)

curriculum.subject: Biology
curriculum.subtopics: Random sampling; quadrats; transects; population estimation; the capture-recapture method; abundance and distribution; presenting ecological data
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Why Do We Sample?
It is impossible to count every individual in a large population. Instead, biologists use sampling — counting a representative subset and using that to estimate the total.

Good sampling must be:
- Random (to avoid bias)
- Large enough (for accuracy)
- Repeatable (others can do it ...
curriculum.activity: Quadrat Sampling: In the school compound/garden, students use homemade 0.5m × 0.5m quadrats (string and sticks) and random number coordinates. They count dandelions, grass, or another common plant. Record in a table. Calculate mean. Estimate total number in the whole garden area. Compare estimates from different groups.
curriculum.homework: Describe how you would use a quadrat to estimate the number of buttercup plants in a field. Include: how you ensure random placement, what measurements you take, and how you calculate the final estimate. A student catches 30 woodlice, marks them, and releases them. The next day they catch 40 woodlice, of which 6 are marked. Estimate the population size. (5 marks)

curriculum.subject: Biology
curriculum.subtopics: The global water cycle; detailed nitrogen cycle; eutrophication; human impacts on water quality; water treatment; acid rain and its effects
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
The Global Water Cycle
Water is continuously cycled between the atmosphere, land, oceans, and living organisms.

DIAGRAM: The Water Cycle
Ocean/Lake → Evaporation → Water vapour in atmosphere → Condensation (clouds) → Precipitation (rain/snow) → Surface runoff / Groundwater → Rivers → Oceans

 Proce...
curriculum.activity: Eutrophication Simulation: Set up two jars of pond water — add fertiliser to one. Leave in light for 1-2 weeks. Students observe difference in green colour (algal growth). Measure turbidity and pH change. Write a report explaining the process of eutrophication using their observations as evidence.
curriculum.homework: Describe the process of eutrophication in a lake, starting from the addition of fertiliser. Explain why excess nitrates in water lead to the death of fish. Describe THREE stages in the treatment of drinking water and explain what is removed at each stage. (5 marks)

curriculum.subject: Biology
curriculum.subtopics: What are tropisms; phototropism; geotropism (gravitropism); hydrotropism; the role of auxins; plant hormones and their applications (rooting powder, weed killer, ripening)
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
What are Tropisms?
A tropism is a growth response of a plant to a directional stimulus. The growth is TOWARDS or AWAY from the stimulus.

 Type  Stimulus  Positive (towards)  Negative (away) 
--------------------------------------------------
 Phototropism  Light  Shoot grows towards light  Root gro...
curriculum.activity: Phototropism Experiment: Plant bean or cress seeds in small cups. Once germinated, cover from all sides EXCEPT a small hole on one side. Leave for 3-4 days. Students observe bending direction. They then explain the result using the role of auxin. If two groups use different light positions, compare results.
curriculum.homework: Explain how auxin causes a shoot to bend towards a light source. Draw a labelled diagram of the shoot showing the distribution of auxin and resulting cell lengths. Describe ONE practical application of auxins in horticulture or agriculture and explain how it works. (4 marks)

curriculum.subject: Biology
curriculum.subtopics: Innate and adaptive immunity; T-cells and B-cells; antibodies and antigens in detail; memory cells and secondary response; monoclonal antibodies and their applications
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Two Lines of Immune Defence

 1. Innate Immunity (Non-Specific — First and Second Line)
- Does NOT distinguish between specific pathogens
- First line: Skin, mucus, cilia, stomach acid
- Second line: Inflammation response, phagocytosis, fever

 2. Adaptive (Specific) Immunity — Third Line
- Responds...
curriculum.activity: Antibody-Antigen Specificity Model: Students cut 'antigen' shapes from card (different shapes = different antigens). Make matching 'antibody' shapes. Demonstrate that each antibody only fits one antigen. Extend: show how memory cells allow faster response by having two rounds — first without memory cell cards, then with pre-made memory cards that respond immediately.
curriculum.homework: Explain the difference between a T-cell and a B-cell in the immune response. Describe what happens during a primary immune response and why the secondary immune response is faster. Describe ONE application of monoclonal antibodies in medicine and explain how it works. (5 marks)

curriculum.subject: Biology
curriculum.subtopics: Hormones controlling the menstrual cycle (FSH, LH, oestrogen, progesterone); follicle development; ovulation; contraception (hormonal and barrier methods); fertility treatments
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Hormones of the Menstrual Cycle
The menstrual cycle is controlled by FOUR main hormones:

 Hormone  Produced By  Main Effect 
--------------------------------
 FSH (Follicle Stimulating Hormone)  Pituitary gland  Stimulates follicle to develop in ovary; stimulates oestrogen production 
 Oestrogen  O...
curriculum.activity: Hormone Cycle Graph Annotation: Provide students with a blank menstrual cycle graph showing 28 days on the x-axis. Students draw and label the changes in FSH, LH, oestrogen, and progesterone levels based on the cycle description. They mark ovulation, menstruation, and the fertile period. Then write a short explanation of what would happen if no fertilisation occurs.
curriculum.homework: Explain how FSH, LH, oestrogen, and progesterone interact to control the menstrual cycle. Describe what happens to each hormone level from day 1 to day 28 if no pregnancy occurs. Explain the mechanism by which the combined oral contraceptive pill prevents pregnancy. (6 marks)

curriculum.subject: Biology
curriculum.subtopics: Structure of neurones in detail; synaptic transmission; the brain in detail; drugs and the synapse; sensory receptors; the eye and vision
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Detailed Neurone Structure

DIAGRAM: Motor Neurone
- Cell body (soma): Contains nucleus and organelles
- Dendrites: Short extensions receive signals from other neurones
- Axon: Long fibre that conducts impulse away from cell body
- Myelin sheath: Fatty insulating layer produced by Schwann cells; spe...
curriculum.activity: Blind Spot Location: Students hold a pencil at arm's length, close their right eye, and stare at a cross mark on paper. Slowly move paper closer until the pencil disappears (falls on blind spot). Then test accommodation by looking at a near object then a distant one quickly — notice how eyes adjust. Discuss implications for everyday life.
curriculum.homework: Describe the events at a synapse when a nerve impulse arrives. Explain why the impulse can only travel in ONE direction across a synapse. Describe how the eye accommodates to see a near object compared to a distant object. (5 marks)

curriculum.subject: Biology
curriculum.subtopics: Innate vs learned behaviour; types of learned behaviour (habituation, conditioning, imprinting, insight learning); social behaviour; communication in animals; foraging behaviour
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Types of Behaviour

 Innate (Instinctive) Behaviour
- Present from birth — not learned
- Genetically determined — same in all members of the species
- Does not require experience or trial and error
- Examples:
  - Spider spinning a web
  - Newborn baby's sucking reflex
  - Monarch butterfly migratio...
curriculum.activity: Habituation Experiment: Students lightly touch a snail's tentacle or shell. Record response. Repeat at 30-second intervals. Tally when the snail stops responding. Plot number of 'responses' vs touch number. Discuss: is this evidence of learning? How does this benefit the snail? Compare with a classmate's data.
curriculum.homework: Distinguish between innate and learned behaviour, giving ONE example of each. Describe the difference between classical and operant conditioning. Give an example of each. Explain what imprinting is and describe a study that demonstrated it. Why is insight learning considered the most advanced form of learning? (6 marks)

curriculum.subject: Biology
curriculum.subtopics: Intensive vs organic farming; use of pesticides, fertilisers, and herbicides; biological pest control; fish farming; fermentation technology; using microorganisms in food production
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Feeding the World — Challenges
The world population is growing rapidly. Producing enough food requires maximising crop yields while minimising environmental damage.

 Farming Methods

 Intensive Farming
- Aims to maximise yield per unit of land
- Uses: high-yield crop varieties, chemical fertilisers...
curriculum.activity: Yoghurt Making: Heat milk to 85°C, cool to 43°C. Add a tablespoon of plain live yoghurt (contains *Lactobacillus*). Keep warm for 6-8 hours (wrap in towel or keep in warm place). Students observe changes in texture and taste. Measure pH at start and end. Discuss: why does it set? Why does it taste sour?
curriculum.homework: Explain how bread-making uses fermentation. What role does yeast play? What gas is produced and what effect does it have? Describe ONE advantage and ONE disadvantage of biological pest control compared to using pesticides. Describe the conditions that need to be controlled in an industrial fermenter and explain why each condition is important. (5 marks)

curriculum.subject: Biology
curriculum.subtopics: Integrating homeostasis, hormones, nervous system; applied problems in health, medicine and agriculture; exam-style questions; synoptic links across Year 2 Term 2 topics
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Integrating What You Have Learned
This week brings together the major themes from Term 2, Year 2. The key unifying idea is coordination and control — how living organisms detect and respond to changes to maintain their survival.

 Quick Review Table

 Topic  Key Concept  Key Terms 
-----------------...
curriculum.activity: Topic Web Challenge: Give students a large sheet of paper and all the key terms from Term 2. They create a concept map, connecting related terms with arrows and brief explanations of the link. The best maps show unexpected connections between different topics. Share with the class and discuss.
curriculum.homework: Choose ONE disease and write a structured account covering: (a) the pathogen that causes it, (b) how it is transmitted, (c) how the immune system responds, (d) how a vaccine could be used to prevent it, and (e) one social or environmental factor that affects its spread. (6 marks)

curriculum.subject: Biology
curriculum.subtopics: Pharmacology basics; legal and illegal drugs; effects of specific drugs on the nervous system; addiction and withdrawal; effects on the liver and brain; drugs in sport
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
What is a Drug?
A drug is any substance that alters the normal functioning of the body or mind.

 Categories of Drugs

 Category  Examples  Legal Status 
------------------------------
 Medicinal  Antibiotics, insulin, aspirin, antidepressants  Legal (prescribed or OTC) 
 Recreational  Alcohol, nico...
curriculum.activity: Drug Effects Analysis: Give students information cards about 4-5 different drugs. Students categorise them (stimulant, depressant, analgesic, hallucinogen), identify their effect on a specific neurotransmitter or receptor, and list one health risk. Students present their findings and discuss the difference between medicinal and recreational use.
curriculum.homework: Explain what is meant by drug addiction and tolerance. Describe the long-term effects of alcohol on the liver. Explain why anabolic steroids are banned in sport, giving both health reasons and ethical reasons. What is the difference between a stimulant and a depressant drug? Give an example of each. (5 marks)

curriculum.subject: Biology
curriculum.subtopics: Cancer biology; causes of cancer; types of tumour; cancer screening and prevention; the relationship between lifestyle and cancer; STIs and prevention
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
What is Cancer?
Cancer is a disease caused by uncontrolled cell division. Normal cells have mechanisms to control growth; cancer cells lose this control.

 Causes of Cancer (Risk Factors)

 Type  Examples 
---------------
 Genetic  Inherited mutations (e.g., BRCA1/2 genes → breast cancer risk) 
 Car...
curriculum.activity: Cancer Risk Assessment: Provide students with a hypothetical 'patient profile' — age, diet, smoking status, family history, UV exposure. Students identify their cancer risk factors and for each one, suggest a specific lifestyle change or screening test. Discuss the difference between risk factors and guarantees.
curriculum.homework: Explain the difference between a benign and a malignant tumour. Describe HOW UV radiation can cause skin cancer (link to mutations and cell cycle). Explain why HIV infection eventually leads to AIDS — describe what happens to the immune system. Suggest THREE ways a person can reduce their risk of developing cancer. (6 marks)

curriculum.subject: Biology
curriculum.subtopics: Measuring biodiversity; indicator species; habitat management; reforestation and rewilding; international conservation efforts; sustainable development goals; climate change and ecology
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Measuring Biodiversity
Biodiversity has two main components:
1. Species richness: The NUMBER of different species in an area
2. Species evenness: How evenly distributed individuals are among species

Simpson's Diversity Index (D):
D = 1 − [Σn(n-1) / N(N-1)]
Where: n = number of individuals of each s...
curriculum.activity: Biodiversity Survey: In groups, students sample a small area of the school grounds using quadrats. Count each species of plant or invertebrate found. Calculate species richness (number of species). Discuss: Is an area with 5 species of 100 individuals each MORE diverse than one with 10 species but 190 from one species? Use Simpson's Index to compare.
curriculum.homework: Explain what is meant by biodiversity and describe ONE way of measuring it. Explain why lichen can be used as an indicator of air quality. Describe TWO strategies that can be used to conserve an endangered species in the wild (in situ) and TWO strategies used outside the natural habitat (ex situ). (6 marks)

curriculum.subject: Biology
curriculum.subtopics: Fossil record in depth; comparative anatomy; molecular evidence for evolution; speciation; Hardy-Weinberg principle (introduction); Lamarck vs Darwin
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Evidence for Evolution — In Depth

 1. The Fossil Record
- Fossils form when organisms die in conditions that prevent decay (underwater sediment, tar, ice, amber)
- Found in sedimentary rock layers (strata) — older layers deeper
- Show transitional forms (e.g., Archaeopteryx — shows features of both...
curriculum.activity: Homologous Bones Investigation: Provide students with diagrams of the 'arms' of five different animals (human, whale, horse, bat, frog). Students colour the corresponding bones the same colour across all five animals. Then answer: What does this tell us about the evolutionary relationship between these animals? How does this evidence support evolution?
curriculum.homework: Describe THREE different types of evidence for evolution, giving a specific example of each. Explain the difference between Lamarck's and Darwin's explanations for the long neck of a giraffe. Define the term 'species'. Explain how geographical isolation can lead to the formation of two new species. (6 marks)

curriculum.subject: Biology
curriculum.subtopics: Fetal development stage by stage; birth and the hormones of labour; breast feeding; sexually transmitted infections (revisited — prevention focus); family planning
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Fetal Development — Stages

First Trimester (0–3 months):
- Week 1–2: Zygote → blastocyst → implantation
- Week 3–4: Embryo forms (3 germ layers); heart begins to beat (week 4)
- Week 5–8: Limb buds appear; face forms; all major organs begin to develop
- Week 9–12: Embryo now called FETUS; 9cm long;...
curriculum.activity: Human Development Timeline: Students create a timeline on a long strip of paper divided into 40 weeks. At each key developmental milestone, they draw a small labelled sketch (e.g., heart beating — week 4; limb buds — week 6; first movements felt — week 18; lung maturity — week 36). Display and discuss the importance of fetal care at each stage.
curriculum.homework: Describe the main developments that take place in the fetus during the FIRST trimester of pregnancy. Explain the role of oxytocin in childbirth. Why is this an example of positive feedback rather than negative feedback? State TWO advantages of breastfeeding for the baby. (5 marks)

curriculum.subject: Biology
curriculum.subtopics: Polygenic inheritance; incomplete dominance; environmental influence on genotype; genetic testing and screening; gene therapy; cloning — natural and artificial
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Polygenic Inheritance
Most characteristics are controlled by MORE than one gene — this is called polygenic inheritance.

Examples: Height, skin colour, intelligence, body weight

Features of polygenic traits:
- Show continuous variation (bell-shaped distribution)
- Many possible phenotypes (not just...
curriculum.activity: Polygenic Skin Colour Model: Give each student a 'genetic lottery' — flip 6 coins (representing 6 alleles from 3 genes for skin colour). Count 'dark' alleles (heads). Class records their totals. Plot a frequency histogram of the class distribution. Observe the bell-shaped curve. Discuss: Why does polygenic inheritance produce continuous variation?
curriculum.homework: Explain the difference between incomplete dominance and codominance. Give an example of each. Explain why many polygenic characteristics (e.g., height) show a normal distribution in a population. Describe the technique used to clone Dolly the sheep. Give ONE ethical concern about reproductive cloning. (6 marks)

curriculum.subject: Biology
curriculum.topic: Human Biology — The Kidneys and Osmoregulation (Advanced)
curriculum.subtopics: Detailed nephron structure; tubular reabsorption; secretion; kidney disease; dialysis; kidney transplant; control of urine composition
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
The Nephron — The Functional Unit of the Kidney
Each kidney contains about ONE MILLION nephrons. Each nephron is a tiny tube that carries out the three stages of urine formation.

DIAGRAM: Detailed Nephron Structure
Components and their locations:

 Nephron Structure  Location  Function 
-----------...
curriculum.activity: Dialysis Machine Simulation: Students simulate haemodialysis using visking tubing (permeable to small molecules) filled with a mixture representing blood with waste products (urea solution + starch). Place in clean dialysis fluid. After 30 min, test the fluid outside for urea (urease test → ammonia → pH indicator) and starch. Discuss clinical applications.
curriculum.homework: Draw a labelled diagram of a nephron. Describe the three main processes that occur in the nephron to produce urine. Explain how ADH controls the concentration of urine produced. Compare dialysis and a kidney transplant as treatments for kidney failure — give ONE advantage and ONE disadvantage of each. (6 marks)

curriculum.subject: Biology
curriculum.subtopics: Variables (independent, dependent, control); reliability and validity; experimental design; graphing skills; analysis and evaluation; writing scientific reports; key practical investigations
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Scientific Method Review

 Types of Variables
 Variable  Definition  Example (enzyme experiment) 
----------------------------------------------
 Independent  What you CHANGE deliberately  Temperature 
 Dependent  What you MEASURE as a result  Rate of reaction (bubbles/min) 
 Control  What you keep ...
curriculum.activity: Planning an Investigation: Present students with a biological question (e.g., 'Does the amount of light affect the rate of photosynthesis in pondweed?'). Students independently write: hypothesis, list of variables (IV, DV, control), step-by-step method, prediction of results, and how they would present data. Peer review each other's plans using a checklist.
curriculum.homework: Design an experiment to test whether the temperature of water affects the rate of germination of mung bean seeds. Include: hypothesis, independent variable, dependent variable, control variables, method (at least 5 steps), expected results, and how you would present your data. (6 marks)

curriculum.subject: Biology
curriculum.subtopics: Comprehensive review of Year 2 topics; connections between topics; past-paper style questions; Cambridge assessment preparation; key vocabulary revision

 3  DNA and Protein Synthesis  Base pairing, transcription, translation

curriculum.activity: Cambridge-Style Mock Assessment: Conduct a timed 60-minute mock paper with questions covering Year 2 topics. Include a mix of: multiple choice, structured questions, extended writing, and a practical/data interpretation question. Mark using a mark scheme. Students score their own paper and identify their three weakest areas for final revision.
curriculum.homework: Produce a personal revision guide for the TWO topics you found hardest this year. For each topic: draw a detailed concept map, write 5 key definitions, write 3 exam-style questions with model answers, and draw any important diagrams. Bring to the final lesson for peer review.

curriculum.subject: Biology
curriculum.subtopics: Complete two-year curriculum review; exam technique; past paper practice; personal revision planning; celebration of learning
curriculum.lesson_notes_preview:
Complete Two-Year Biology — Master Summary

 Core Biological Themes
Everything in biology can be organised under FIVE master themes:

1. STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION
Every structure (cell organelle, tissue, organ, ecosystem component) exists because it performs a function. Structure is always adapted to f...
curriculum.activity: Biology Celebration and Reflection: Students complete a personal biology journal entry: 'The most interesting thing I learned in biology this year was...'. Each student then creates a 'Biology in my life' card — identifying THREE biological concepts they now recognise in their daily life (digestion, breathing, nutrition, disease prevention, etc.). Share and celebrate. Optionally, conduct a fun biology quiz tournament as a class.
curriculum.homework: Final Exam Revision: Complete FIVE past Cambridge Lower Secondary Biology questions from different topic areas. For each answer, write a brief evaluation: 'What did I do well?' and 'What would I improve?' This reflection will prepare you for your final examination and for IGCSE Biology.
